one_line_takeaway: A two-stage, physically-parameterized (CMA-ES-optimized) rain-and-illumination adversarial attack degrades zero-shot CLIP classification accuracy by up to 62 points and, without any task-specific re-optimization, transfers to cause larger captioning and VQA score drops than the prior ITA illumination-attack baseline on most tested VLMs.
key_finding: On zero-shot classification (Table 1, Top-1 accuracy %, clean->attacked): OpenCLIP ViT-B/16 97->35 (down 62), Meta-CLIP ViT-L/14 98->42 (down 56), EVA-CLIP ViT-G/14 98->58 (down 40), OpenAI CLIP ViT-L/14 93->54 (down 39). The proposed method beats ITA on the first three backbones (ITA drops were 51/34/14 points respectively) but ITA slightly outperforms it on OpenAI CLIP ViT-L/14 (ITA down 42 vs. proposed down 39), which the authors attribute to that backbone's stronger robustness to local structural perturbations. On image captioning (Table 2, GPT-4o judge score drop), the transferred (not re-optimized) attack produces larger drops than ITA across all six captioning models, e.g. InstructBLIP (FlanT5XL) 76.50->42.86 (down 33.64) vs ITA's down 13.30, and BLIP-2 (FlanT5XL) down 29.63 vs ITA's down 11.88. On VQA (Table 3, GPT-4o binary-judge accuracy %), the transferred attack again exceeds ITA on all six models, e.g. OpenFlamingo 45.00->14.00 (down 31.00) vs ITA down 26.00, BLIP-2 (FlanT5XL) 54.00->22.00 (down 32.00) vs ITA down 21.00. Component ablation (Table 4, attack-success-style metric) shows removing multi-scale raindrops causes the largest performance drop (e.g. Ours 62 on OpenCLIP ViT-B/16 falls to 12 without raindrops), while removing illumination modulation causes a smaller but still meaningful drop (36 on OpenCLIP ViT-B/16), confirming both components are complementary. Stage-1 hyperparameter ablation: with large lambda_reg^(1) (1 or 10^-1) and small lambda_perc^(1) (10^-3 or 10^-2), Stage 1 alone reaches up to 12.7% attack success on Meta-CLIP ViT-L/14, and final (Stage-2) success under those settings reaches 45.0%; overall success ranges 26%-57.7% even when Stage-1 success is low, indicating Stage 1 mainly provides favorable initialization rather than directly driving final attack success. Population-size ablation: attack success improves as CMA-ES population grows from 5 to 15, then plateaus with diminishing returns beyond 15.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is a two-stage, semantics-decoupled adversarial attack that generates physically plausible rainy-weather images to degrade Vision-Language Model (VLM) robustness. Stage 1 optimizes a single low-dimensional global mixing weight w_p that linearly blends a fixed global rain layer into the clean image (I^(1) = (1-w_p)I + w_p*R_fix), minimizing the margin between the ground-truth semantic similarity and the strongest competing class, regularized by VGG perceptual loss and a quadratic penalty on w_p, in order to pre-condition/relax the embedding-space decision boundary. Stage 2 then uses CMA-ES (a gradient-free, black-box optimizer, since the physical rain/illumination parameter space is non-differentiable) to jointly optimize a 6-parameter raindrop control vector theta_r = (intensity, density, length, width, direction, blur kernel) with multi-scale superposition, plus a multi-source 2D-Gaussian illumination field (position, intensity, radius per light source) applied multiplicatively as a gain map, subject to a margin-based attack loss, Top-K-restricted VGG perceptual loss, SSIM realism regularization, and illumination-range/level regularizers. The authors claim to be 'the first to systematically study the robustness of VLMs under rainy conditions' with a focus on semantic (not pixel-level) degradation, evaluated via cross-task transfer (attack optimized only for zero-shot classification, then reused unmodified for captioning and VQA).
threat_model: White/gray-box on the CLIP-style image/text encoders used to generate the attack (paper accesses fv/ft embeddings and their cosine similarity Sk(I)=<fv(I),ft(Tk)> to compute the attack loss and optimize with CMA-ES), producing perturbations constrained to a physically interpretable, non-pixel parameter space (rain and illumination parameters) rather than unconstrained pixel noise, with perceptual (VGG feature) and structural (SSIM) naturalness constraints. Attacks generated against the classification embedding space are then transferred black-box (no further optimization) to downstream captioning and VQA models, evaluated by GPT-4o as an LLM-as-Judge rather than exact-match metrics. This is a physical/realistic-corruption adversarial-robustness threat model (akin to Shadow Attack, Natural Light Attack, ITA), not a jailbreak/prompt-injection/data-poisoning threat model.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: the study covers only rainy conditions as a representative weather scenario and does not explicitly address other complex environmental factors (fog, haze, snow); and while downstream-task impact is evaluated, there is room for deeper investigation into finer-grained perturbation mechanisms and interactions among different environmental factors. Additional limitations evident from the text: the attack is optimized only against the classification-stage CLIP embedding and merely transferred (not re-optimized) to captioning/VQA models, so captioning/VQA results reflect transfer robustness rather than a task-specific attack; correctness for captioning and VQA is judged by a single LLM-as-Judge (GPT-4o) with no reported human-agreement validation of that judge; the method underperforms the ITA baseline on one of four classification backbones (OpenAI CLIP ViT-L/14); no defense or mitigation is proposed or evaluated (the authors explicitly defer this to future work); and the paper reports no formal robustness bound or certified defense evaluation, only empirical accuracy/score drops on a fixed 300-image, 30-category COCO subset.

datasets_used: MS COCO (300 images, 30 semantic categories, following the ITA evaluation protocol; category list in Appendix B.1/Table 5)
quantitative_results: Zero-shot classification Top-1 accuracy drop (Table 1): OpenCLIP ViT-B/16 97%->35% (down 62); Meta-CLIP ViT-L/14 98%->42% (down 56); EVA-CLIP ViT-G/14 98%->58% (down 40); OpenAI CLIP ViT-L/14 93%->54% (down 39)

Image captioning GPT-4o-judge score drop (Table 2, transferred attack): LLaVA-1.5 78.60->56.14 (down 22.46); LLaVA-1.6 72.10->46.58 (down 25.52); OpenFlamingo 70.20->46.10 (down 24.10); BLIP-2 (FlanT5XL ViT-L) 75.10->51.77 (down 23.33); BLIP-2 (FlanT5XL) 74.96->45.33 (down 29.63); InstructBLIP (FlanT5XL) 76.50->42.86 (down 33.64)

VQA GPT-4o-judge accuracy drop (Table 3, transferred attack): LLaVA-1.5 68.00%->38.00% (down 30.00); LLaVA-1.6 64.00%->37.00% (down 27.00); OpenFlamingo 45.00%->14.00% (down 31.00); BLIP-2 (FlanT5XL ViT-L) 63.00%->30.00% (down 33.00); BLIP-2 (FlanT5XL) 54.00%->22.00% (down 32.00); InstructBLIP (FlanT5XL) 68.00%->38.00% (down 30.00)

Component ablation (Table 4, attack metric on classification backbones): full method 62/56/40/39 (OpenCLIP/Meta-CLIP/EVA-CLIP/OpenAI CLIP); w/o multi-scale raindrops 12/17/13/20; w/o illumination modulation 36/45/31/27; ITA baseline 51/34/14/42

Stage-1 hyperparameter ablation: peak Stage-1-only attack success 12.7% (Meta-CLIP ViT-L/14, large lambda_reg^(1), small lambda_perc^(1)); corresponding final (Stage-2) success up to 45.0%; overall success range across settings 26%-57.7%
Population-size ablation: attack success improves as CMA-ES population grows from 5 to 15, then plateaus with diminishing gains beyond 15
baselines_compared: Illumination Transformation Attack (ITA, Liu et al. 2025)
